sentences.text: Yes.
When we arrived in Auschwitz, as I said, it-- it took the families in big trucks and the women in another section.
But I don't know where the women went.
Was another locker, eventually I found out.
We were walking through Birkenau.
Auschwitz was a station, but I got into Birkenau, not Auschwitz.
I mean, I went to a labor camp what it says on the front as you're walking in, it says Arbeit macht das leben frei.
That means work makes you-- your life easy.
Anyway, we got into there.
So they took us into-- we were waiting, waiting, waiting almost get in 2, 3 o'clock in the morning, you know.
And there were some miserable April nights, sort of a small rain hitting-- hitting us.
We were sitting outside.
Finally, they took us into a big barrack, big barrack.
And they said, OK, all the-- whatever you got in your pockets, put them in this basket.
If anybody found something, they will be shot right on the spot.
So everybody got scared.
Watches, whatever they had, they throw into the big basket.
And we are lined up to put us a number.
So-- no.
Yeah.
They put us-- put us a number.
And I was with my two brothers.
And I got my number is 114191, which is-- [POINTING] And my second brother had 114192, and my oldest brother had 114193.
I was the one who had the courage to go first, so I got my first number.
Actually, it wasn't painful.
We only just-- a little bit of blood came out because they were doing it with a big needle, you know.
It wasn't like a machine like you do here, artistic work.
Just a big needle, just punching numbers on it until they get their-- the number they want.
A prison-- a prisoner who was ahead of us.
You know, was not a Greek, actually, it was a Polish, was a Polack.
The Polish people were there ahead of us.
You know, they were still taking them before we got there.
Polish.
Well, we haven't had a chance to talk to them yet.
We were just-- we couldn't even speak to them anyway.
First of all, we couldn't speak Yiddish.
We couldn't speak German.
We were a bunch of dummies.
We spoke Spanish and Greek or French or Hebrew.
This is the languages the-- the Greek Jews knew.
French, Greek, Hebrew, and Spanish.
This is only four languages was popular between the Greek Jews.
So we had Polacks over there and we had Germans.
We couldn't get a-- you know, couldn't understand a word.
But I happened to be-- what do you call-- one Greek Jew which he came from Polish background, but he used to live in Greece.
And he was one of the first ones who got there.
And he spoke half Yiddish and half German.
And he was the guy who was at the barrack where they were putting us the-- the tattoos.
So they put us the tattoos, and all of a sudden we asked him, I says, Leon.
Leon Yachiel was his name, which later he became a big-- what do you call-- a big officer in the-- in the-- in Israel-- in the Israeli army.
I-- I met him after the war.
And we asked him, Yachiel, what's happened to our families?
And he was rude.
We wanted to kill him.
Well, you see, you see those chimneys up there?
That's where your families are.
They're all burned.
It was sort of a-- they wanted to kill him.
Because whoever believed that-- that a thing like this can happen?
We just came from home, you know.
And all of a sudden the guy-- he was telling the truth, though.
The guy was-- Did you believe him when he was telling you this?
No, we didn't.
We didn't.
At the moment we didn't.
A couple days later we did because we smelled the-- what do you call-- the skin of the-- of the chimneys.
They were coming, the smoke was a smell of burned-- what do you call it-- skin.
But we didn't-- we didn't believe it for a while.
So we-- they tattoo us pretty good, and they put us back into line.
And they took us into a bathhouse and told us to take all our clothes off and we should only keep the big-- the shoes and the belt.
And I happened to have a nice pair of boots from a Greek officer that I bought in Greece before I left for the-- what do you call-- for the-- Ghetto.
The ghetto.
And I was wearing those boots.
And I had a nice pair of pants with like a breeches, you know, like breeches inside the boots and a nice jacket, you know, well dressed.
So that's what-- the only thing I had with me.
And I had a-- a carton of cigarettes, 100 cigarettes.
But I didn't give it to when they say to give everything.
So when I went into the bath, I took the carton of cigarettes.
I didn't smoke at the time.
And I put into show-- my brother smoked and my brother's friends smoked.
So I took the whole carton of cigarettes, and I put them into the boots, you know.
And we went into the bath.
First of all, they shaved us.
They shaved us all the way.
They left us a little Lause Strasse.
Lause Strasse means a-- a-- a street to go to the-- the lice to go through.
Lause Strasse.

That was the name of the-- so they shaved us pretty good all over, and then they put us some kind of a-- a-- burn-- alcohol or something into, you know, where we had a little bit of hair left, and we went in to take a bath.
Took a bath, a cold water bath.
So we come out, and they give us some different clothes.
One guy's got pants that size.

Another guy so-- so finally we started to change them around till you get the right-- you know, everybody was changing themselves, because one was big enough because they didn't-- they give us clothes from other prisoners.
Of-- whatever they had, they give us.
Where did you think these clothes were coming from?
From the people who were gassed, from the-- what do you call it-- from the crematoriums, you know, different.
And finally, we got out of there.
A whole night outside.
God, we wanted to rest.
You know, we were five days on the train.
And already took us about 48 hours by the time we got the numbers.
We got into the camp, we got the numbers, we went through the steam-- to the room-- to the showers, here and there.
It's already seven days, you know.
We were so tired.
And finally, they put us in a block.
Block 21 was my block.
They put us over there.
Take a look.
Seven people, there was three rows on-- in-- in-- in the barrack.
One, two, three.
Seven rows of seven-- three rows, seven people per row.
And they gave us a few blankets there to cover.
So me and my two brothers, so then my two brothers, they grabbed me, they put me in the middle, you know.
And finally, we went-- we slept.
You know, we were almost dead.
And at 5 o'clock in the morning, all of a sudden, the [GERMAN] is the guy who-- who's in charge of the block with two helpers, with the big hoses started hitting us.
Wake up, wake up, wake up.
So we got-- I got up, and I didn't know what's going on.
So I got in to the other door.
They got up to about 30 or 40 of us, and I was in it.

My first job was to load up dead bodies, they were from-- from labor, you know, like they went to work, laborers from the hospital, dead bodies were all outside to load them up into trucks to take them to the crematorium, to burn them, you see.
That was my first night in concentration camp.
I came back, and I was crying and crying and crying.
So finally, this is it.
This is the life of the camp.
They kept us about a week there, you know, in quarantine, you know.
They'll find places where to send us to work.
I was-- they put me to work with my-- with my two brothers.
Just nonsense work, just strictly nonsense.
Just cutting a square of grass, you know.
And they were taking the grass, put them to another place.
They were-- we didn't have-- they weren't getting anything.
Just strictly work that wasn't-- we weren't producing anything.
They weren't getting our money's worth, you understand?
So they gave us a-- in the morning a colored coffee, they call it, you know.
We used to-- they give us a pot, a round pot, a brown colored one, and a spoon.
And in the morning they give us some color-- colored coffee, but some color, that was it.
Lunchtime at work they-- there was a soup.
And at night, when we used to come from back from work, they used to give us a piece of bread, some margarine, and a spoon of-- what do you call-- of marmalade.
That was the beginning.
That's where my beginning of the camp started.
But you know, they didn't take too long.
And then Sundays, instead of having a day of rest, they used to take us-- we used to have a jacket, a prisoner's jacket, a striped jacket, you know.
Turn it over.
You put the sleeves this way and carry the-- the back of the jacket into the front.
And we used to carry earth, one shovel of earth from here till about 100 yards, just dump it.
That was the work they-- they put us on a Sunday.
But it was useless.
Just to keep us, you know, occupied.
And it was really, really very bad.
Of course, we were all greenhorns.
We just came from home.
We didn't know those things.
None of us saw a thing like this.
We didn't expect to have it.
But we had to manage.
The guards.
We didn't have too many guards.
You know, the worst people were the foremans that we had, the-- the-- Polish Gentiles were kapos.
That was-- the guards were only-- what do you call-- in big-- what do you call those things?
Pillars.
You know, they used to put is in a-- in a section to work.
Used to be four guards, that's all.
And they used to have those-- the gates on top.
They used to watch us--
Watch tower.
They used to there and watching us, that's all.
But the-- the people who were worst, it was the kapos.
That was the-- I don't know why they were doing it because we were Jews, I guess.
And--
Hitting you.
If you don't work good enough, they hitting you.
So that kept me for a while, you know, working.
Then my brother, my-- what do you call it-- my oldest brother didn't last too long.
He got sick, and he went into the hospital.
And there every month we used to have a selection.
Selection means you get undressed and you stay naked completely outside, and a German doctor will come in.
He will do with his finger this way and the finger that way.
If the finger that way is to-- back to work, and the other finger is to the crematorium.
But I was young, still strong from home, you know.
I was OK.
And then I got close with two kapos, Polish kapos, that they used to sleep on the-- what do you call it-- they used to have their own-- two of them instead of seven of us.
Two of them on the top level.
And they were Gentile.
And I used to make their bed, you know, in the morning.
And they used to get packages from home.
And they used to give me whatever they didn't want.
But I wouldn't eat chaza.
I wouldn't eat--
Pig.
--pork or pig and all that.
So I used to trade it in, you know, and get something else for it so.
And I-- I was good.
And then I started working for this kapo in-- in this kommando.
And it was-- I was OK, doing all right.
I didn't work very hard.
I used to have a Polish meister.
Meister means-- Polish meister is a guy who knows how to build, like a builder.
They-- those were volunteers.
They were not prisoners.
They used to come from the city and work.
And they used to have the prisoners.
And they used to tell us what to do, you know, like-- and I used to mix the cement for him, you know, I used to-- so it was OK.
It wasn't that bad.
And this kapo, the kapo that I had, he helped me, you know.
I didn't work very hard.
No, we didn't have any people from the city.
No people from the city.
Was no people from the city in the-- in the camp.
We had no people from the city.
Was strictly everything in-- in a conclude place.
They were making canals, you know, like those canals and building things.
And they used to bring a few Polish volunteer workers.
They used to get paid, I guess.
And they used to have us prisoners to help them.
You know, like to do the work for them.
By those people?
About OK.
They all wanted things, you know.
They-- they wanted different things from us, but we didn't have much to give them.
But we used to do a lot of black market with the Polacks.
With the-- like my kapo.
They used to do a lot of business back and forth, you know.
In between them, you know, like they used to bring food into camp.
They used to bring a lot of things.
And I used to carry them in camp for my kapo, you see.
And then about-- my brother got sick.
Avraham.
He went to the hospital, and he came out of the hospital.
He was really skinny.
They put him into a quarantine block, block 31.
There was strictly-- which I wasn't aware of it.
And he was there.
But I tried to help him.
I already had connections with my kapo.
And he used to bring him some milk from the gypsies, the gypsy locker.
We used to have the gypsy camp next to ours.
And the gypsies were good.
They had everything.
Till in the end they burned them all.
But while they were there, they were with their families.
They were with everything.
And I used to do transactions through the-- through the wire fence.
And I used to have some bread, some milk.
And I was trying to give it to my brother.
Because I was block 21, and he was block 31.
So it was quite a walk to go over.
And I used to go there and give him, you know, make sure that he gets some energy, you know.
Because he wasn't working.
He was strictly waiting there.
And sure enough, it didn't take long.
Another selection came in.
So I was out on the line again, so I was OK, back to work.
And they took the whole block where my-- where my brother was.
You know, 90% of them they took.
And I to go-- I go to the block.
I says to my brother, they took your number?
He says, yes.
I start crying because I knew what was going on.
So I went to my kapo, can you help him?
Please.
My brother, they took the number.
So they went over here.
They went to the block at-- so the guy was in charge of this particular block.
He went to him.
He was a nice Gentile-- my kapo was a nice man, a handsome man.
He went to this guy, he says, listen, this number so and so, 114192.
Takes a look and says, yeah, the number is gone already.
He went to the-- to the [GERMAN], it was the office, you know, the German-- couldn't do nothing.
And in the middle of the night, midnight, 12:00, they took him on foot, which is the worst thing, you know.
When my parents got burned and killed, they didn't know if they were coming or going.
You know, they just went to take a bath, you know.
And all of a sudden, in 10-- 10, 15, 20 minutes were all dead.
But those people, they took them on foot to-- to the crematorium.
And it was the worst thing anybody know already.
By that time, we knew what those chimneys was, you know.
We knew exactly what the chimneys-- so and then before they got into the doors from the crematorium, a lot of people wouldn't want to go in.
So they shot them in the doorways, you know.
All around so we-- we found out.
So that's when I lost my brother.
But prior to that, my older-- my second brother was a selection.
But that selection was to take some workers to-- what do you call it-- to another camp, Camp Buna, the name Buna.
So he was selected.
I was not selected.
So I lost my brother.
So he went.
They took him away.
And he went to Buna.
So a lot-- a lot of Greek Jews went to Buna.
So-- and then I lost contact with him.
I never knew what happened to him.
And I was alone now in Birkenau.
And I was actually-- I couldn't say I suffered as much the other prisoners.
Of course, I had my lice and I had the-- the times, the changing times.
I had my own share.
But people were dying.
And I wasn't-- I wasn't, you know-- while I was working for this kapo, I had no fear of dying, you know, because, you know, I had something to eat.
And I was working with a-- a guy on, you know, with a whip.
But-- but it did happen.
While I was cutting grass, squares of grass, was with a fellow prisoner, also Greek Jew.
And all of a sudden, one of the-- what do you call it-- the guards, I don't know, 300 yards away and takes the-- he takes the-- the rifle-- pfft-- he shots him right next to me.
He dropped dead, you know.
And all of a sudden, I got scared.
I started shoveling, shoveling you know.
Like, I didn't know what to do.
I was-- I was numb.
When I see the man I was just talking two minutes before, I was talking to the guy.
All of a sudden he's dead on the-- on the ground.
So that was one of the bad experience that I-- that I had while I was working in Birkenau.
You know, in-- Did you know the name of this man?
No, I don't remember, dear.
No, I don't remember.
He was a little older than me.
He was older than me.
So I don't remember.
In Birkenau?
Oh, Birkenau was the biggest brutality that you ever seen.
But I didn't go through it my-- personally.
But I've seen people, first of all, lot of people tried to escape.
No escape out of Birkenau.
They put the dogs at night.
They go over there, and they bring them in.
And you used to hang them right in front of us.
We had to wait about sometimes two, three hours until everybody, you know-- after you're working the whole day long, you have to wait two, three hours till the-- everything is organized.
And they take the guy and they hang them, you know.
Or during the war days, you know, during a day's work, somebody would take your number.
You will-- for some reason or other you didn't do the work right and so on.
They bring you over to the-- when you come into the camp, they call your number.

Put you in a-- in a big bank, a big chair like this, put your head inside, and give you 25 up your ass, you know, with a big-- there was-- quite often, because from different commanders, you know, it was done this way.
And as you mentioned, the music.
The music was when we used to go in the morning and we used to be-- mutze up, mutze up, you know, take your hats, you know, walking, you know.
And we passed through the gates.
And then at night the same thing.
We used to have the music playing as we're coming into-- what do you call?
It was-- if it was no delays, you know, nobody run away or nothing like that.
So we were going to-- we used to go right into the camp, like into our blocks.
And they used to be on the line.
And we used to give us the food.
And I was very fortunate again.
I happened to have the officer's boots, nice, nice, nice, brown, shiny, soft leather boots.
And all of a sudden, a kapo-- no, a blockalteste alteiser from next block saw my boots.
Then he saw me, you know.
Then after he saw me in the line that I got my food, my supper, whatever it was, he calls me, shh, come over.
I got scared.
I don't know what he wants.
Says to me, I want your boots.
I'll give you Russian boots, which is much stronger for the mud that we had in-- in Birkanau.
Another month those boots would have been useless because it was thin, very, very thin leather soles, you know.
But it wouldn't last in-- in-- not in Birkenau.
So he says, I want those boots for my girlfriend.
He had a girlfriend in the locker, in the next locker, which was a women's locker, you know.
I says, OK.
I says, you bring me a good pair of Polish or Russian good, heavy boots.
They was perfect.
And then he says, you come here every night, I'll give you a-- a-- a meal, a big lot of meal.
But you don't, you come, you stay in the corner here.
When, you know, you finish-- when I finish giving food to the camp, you know, to the guys.
So yeah.
And that was right in the beginning.
That was right in the beginning, the first three weeks that I was there.
My two brothers were with me at that time.
So-- and I used to go over there.
He used to give me enough to eat.
So I used to divide it to my brothers, because I was in-- my stomach shrunk so bad that I couldn't eat much, you know.
I was a young kid.
But my brothers kind of, you know, they were really had appetite.
And for a long time, the blockalteste give me a lot of food, and I was giving it to my brothers.
And next thing you know, I kept going in camp, you know.
It's routine.
Routine, dead people coming daily.
And finally, towards I already was there a few months, I believe it was August, August of '43, a new selection.
We don't know what it is.
So they picked my number.
I start crying.
I go to my kapo.
I says, they took my number.
I don't-- says, stupid.
You go to Warschau.
Go.
You know, you should go.
No, I says, I'm good here.
Because I was-- so-- so bad everyone else.
And I-- I-- I was good there, so I didn't want to move.
No, no, no, he says.
That's where I come from.
I am from Warschau.
Over there there's no gas chambers.
He knew, you know.
You go.
Finally, he made me go.
He pushed me, you know.
He said, you should go.
So OK.
A day later, they took us and took us to Auschwitz.
Auschwitz was like paradise compared to Birkenau.
Auschwitz only sleeping two in a bed.
We were seven in Birkenau.
We were there for only 48 hours.
They give us a bath, and they give us civilian clothes.
And they put a big red-- red paint on the back of the jacket, you know.
But the only thing they give us bad was wooden shoes.
Wooden-- wooden shoes.
That was bad.
That was really bad.
But I found out later that it was bad.
Well, in case you run away so they'll know who you are.
Because in Warschau, there was no electric-- in Warschau ghetto when we got down there, there was no electric wires.
It was just-- what do you call-- a brick wall, you know, are surrounded.
And there were some guards, you know, on top, just a few guards.
It was sort of nothing there.
There was no-- there was nothing, actually.
When we got into Warschau and I start-- they started building our own.
We didn't have even two at the stage.
Just odd barracks.
So they started building the barracks.
And I got started working, bringing potatoes from the-- from outside and carrying by hand to the kitchen for-- for the cooks to start preparing food for us, you see.
Like-- and it was hard work, you know, I was young and it was cold already.
It was--
Yeah, OK.
